name: Florida Causes of Action & Legal Standards index
note: Original index of Florida civil causes of action with elements, statute of limitations (SOL), key defenses, and authority, authored from public Florida law (Florida Standard Jury Instructions, the Florida Statutes, and established case law). Element formulations are the law, not protected expression. SOL per § 95.11 unless noted — VERIFY each against current law (accrual rules, tolling, and amendments vary). 'cat' groups the cause; 'sol_years' is the limitations period; 'basis' is the primary authority class. Title XLI (FUFTA Ch.726 fraudulent transfers + Ch.725 statute of frauds + Ch.727 ABC) ingested 2026-06-09 from leg.state.fl.us — verbatim text in lib/statute-text/title-xli-fufta-excerpts.md.
sol_reference: § 95.11: 5 yr — written contract, mortgage foreclosure; 4 yr — negligence/most torts, oral contract, fraud (from discovery), FDUTPA, property damage, statutory (unless other); 2 yr — defamation, professional/medical malpractice, wrongful death, FCCPA; 5 yr — civil theft (§ 772.17); usury varies. Always verify accrual + tolling.

elements: an express or implied agreement to pay
a promise to pay the stated amount
an unpaid balance on a running account
as a loan (intent to be repaid)
clear intent to primarily and directly benefit the plaintiff
defenses: contract defenses run against the beneficiary
an express contract governs the subject

causes.n: Breach of Implied Covenant of Good Faith & Fair Dealing
elements: exercise of that discretion in bad faith to deprive plaintiff of the contract's benefit
defenses: no breach of an express term to anchor it

elements: reliance forming basis of the bargain

causes.n: Breach of Implied Warranty of Merchantability
elements: a merchant seller of such goods
goods not merchantable/fit for ordinary use

causes.n: Breach of Implied Warranty of Fitness for a Particular Purpose
elements: seller had reason to know the particular purpose
damages proximately caused by the breach

causes.n: Breach of Fiduciary Duty—Aiding and Abetting
elements: an underlying breach of fiduciary duty
defendant's substantial assistance in the breach
plaintiff conferred a benefit (services/goods) at defendant's request
inequity of retention without payment of value
the promisor should expect to induce reliance
a representation of a material fact contrary to a later position
a detrimental change of position from that reliance
a fiduciary relationship or complex transactions
a remedy at law is inadequate
a ground (fraud, mistake, failure of consideration, illegality)
restoration of the status quo (offer to return)
a valid, definite contract (often real estate)
a transfer of property in reliance
one party pays the purchase price
intent that the payor hold the beneficial interest (no gift intended)
plaintiff paid a debt to protect its own interest
the debt was primarily owed by another
no prejudice to others / equities favor
plaintiff wholly without fault but liable
a special relationship with the indemnitor
the indemnitor's fault caused the liability
(for own-negligence indemnity) clear and unequivocal language
plaintiff paid more than its pro-rata share
defenses: comparative-fault apportionment (§ 768.81 largely supplanted joint liability)

elements: a composite of circumstances creating a clear and present danger
defenses: workers'-comp immunity unless gross negligence/intentional tort (§ 440.11)

elements: actual or constructive notice of it
duty to operate with reasonable care
a physical impact (impact rule) OR being in the zone of danger with physical manifestation
made without reasonable care as to truth
to one defendant knew/should know was incompetent/reckless
the employer knew/should have known of the employee's unfitness
employer knew/should have known of the need to supervise
failure to use reasonable care in supervising
inability to prove the underlying claim
defenses: no independent tort vs. a party (sanctions instead)
causes.basis: case law (Martino — limited vs. first parties)

elements: breach of the professional standard of care
the attorney's neglect of a reasonable duty
defenses: SOL accrues at final judgment (redressable harm)

elements: a defect made it unreasonably dangerous
the defect existed when it left defendant
the inadequacy made it unreasonably dangerous
the product deviated from its intended design
existed when it left the manufacturer
plaintiff was lawfully present (public place or lawfully on private property)
defenses: § 767.04 strict liability subject to comparative negligence

elements: an intentional, unlawful threat by word or act
apparent ability to carry it out
creating a well-founded fear of imminent violence
unlawful restraint against the plaintiff's will
awareness or harm from the confinement
a false oral statement of fact
a statement that, on its face, charges a crime, a loathsome disease, professional unfitness, or unchastity
defenses: not per se on its face

elements: juxtaposed to create a false defamatory implication
a false statement about the plaintiff's property/business
a false statement disparaging the plaintiff's title
a prior judicial proceeding commenced by defendant
its bona fide termination in plaintiff's favor
a willful act in the use of process not proper in the regular conduct of the proceeding
defenses: process used for its proper purpose

elements: plaintiff's ownership/right to possession of specific property
defendant's wrongful dominion inconsistent with that right
(for money) an obligation to keep it intact/identifiable
the elements of theft under § 812.014 (knowing, felonious intent to deprive)
a 30-day written pre-suit demand for treble damages
intentional interference with plaintiff's possession of personal property
that would be highly offensive to a reasonable person

causes.n: Invasion of Privacy—Public Disclosure of Private Facts
elements: highly offensive to a reasonable person
defenses: truth is not a defense here but consent is

elements: [NOTE: Florida does NOT recognize false light (Jews for Jesus v. Rapp, 2008) — plead defamation/by-implication instead]
defenses: not a cognizable claim in Florida

causes.n: Tortious Interference with a Contractual Right
elements: intentional and unjustified procurement of its breach
defenses: justification/privilege (e.g., a party or its agent cannot interfere)

causes.n: Tortious Interference with an Advantageous Business Relationship
elements: a business relationship with legal rights

causes.n: Tortious Interference with the Parent-Child Relationship
elements: a parent's right to the child's society

causes.n: Tortious Interference with a Dead Body
elements: plaintiff's right to possess/bury the remains

causes.n: Wrongful Interference with a Testamentary Expectancy

elements: an agreement between two or more parties
to do an unlawful act or a lawful act by unlawful means
defenses: no underlying tort (conspiracy is not independent, except for the 'peculiar power of coercion')

elements: a false statement of material fact
a false statement of material fact inducing the contract
fraudulent conduct in performing the contract (not merely breaching)
abuse of that relationship/breach of a duty
the seller knew of a material defect
not readily observable / unknown to the buyer

causes.n: Florida Deceptive and Unfair Trade Practices Act (FDUTPA)
elements: a deceptive act or unfair practice in trade or commerce
defenses: not deceptive to a reasonable consumer

elements: a rate above the legal maximum (18% / 25% > $500k)
a violation of the Florida Building Code
that the code provision was intended to protect plaintiff
a request for a public record
the trade dress is distinctive/has secondary meaning
defendant's conduct likely to cause public confusion/deception
defendant's use of a confusingly similar mark
plaintiff's right to possession of specific personal property
without consent and without a tenancy
the parcel became landlocked at severance
shut off from a public road
no reasonable practical route except over another's land
an express or implied contract for fees
an understanding the fee is payable from the recovery
the attorney's possession of the client's papers/property
balance of equities/public interest (no bond for permanent)
likelihood of success on the merits
causes.basis: Rule 1.610 — see Title VI injunction template

elements: an unreasonable interference with a right common to the public
(for a private plaintiff) special injury different in kind
a substantial and unreasonable interference by defendant
a joint venture (community of interest, joint control, sharing of profits AND losses)
a valid marriage at the time of injury
a tortious injury to the spouse
defenses: derivative — fails if the primary claim fails
comparative fault of the injured spouse

elements: a significant injury to a minor child
parental loss of the child's companionship/society
a death caused by a wrongful act/negligence/default
that would have entitled the decedent to sue had they lived
a duty (usually medical) to inform of a risk/defect
breach depriving parents of an informed reproductive choice
§ 90.702 (Daubert): qualified expert; reliable principles/methods; reliably applied to sufficient facts; helpful to the trier
a § 768.72 evidentiary proffer BEFORE pleading punitives
intentional misconduct or gross negligence by clear and convincing evidence
a creditor with a claim (arising before OR after the transfer)
a transfer of an asset (or incurring of an obligation) by the debtor

made with actual intent to hinder, delay, or defraud a creditor — proved by the § 726.105(2) badges of fraud (insider, retained control, concealment, prior suit, transfer of substantially all assets, insolvency, not reasonably equivalent value, timing vs. debt)
defenses: good-faith transferee for reasonably equivalent value (§ 726.109(1))
SOL — 4 yr or 1 yr from discovery (§ 726.110)

elements: a transfer/obligation by the debtor WITHOUT receiving reasonably equivalent value

AND either: the debtor's remaining assets were unreasonably small for its business (§ 726.105(1)(b)1) / it intended to incur debts beyond its ability to pay (§ 726.105(1)(b)2); OR (present creditor, § 726.106(1)) the debtor was insolvent then or became insolvent as a result — NO intent required
defenses: reasonably equivalent value given (defeats constructive fraud)

causes.n: Assignment for the Benefit of Creditors (ABC, Ch. 727)
elements: a debtor (assignor) conveys all nonexempt assets to a neutral assignee
a verified petition + the assignment recorded/filed in circuit court (§ 727.104)
the assignee liquidates and distributes to creditors per priorities (§ 727.110-.111); creditors file claims by the bar date (§ 727.108)
defenses: the ABC is itself a fraudulent transfer / sham to defeat a creditor (attack via Ch. 726)
exclusive-forum bar (§ 727.105) cuts against separate creditor suits vs. the assets

standards.note: tests the four corners of the complaint; all well-pleaded facts taken as true; no evidence; de novo on appeal

Florida adopted the FEDERAL/Celotex standard in 2021 — 'no genuine dispute of material fact'; movant may point to the absence of evidence; the prior Florida 'slightest doubt' standard is GONE; 40-day notice
at trial, no legally sufficient evidentiary basis for a reasonable jury to find for the non-movant; evidence viewed for the non-movant

standards.n: Motion for Judgment Notwithstanding the Verdict (JNOV)
standards.note: renewed DV after verdict; same standard; preserves the DV
verdict against the manifest weight of the evidence, or trial error; broad discretion; reviewed for abuse of discretion

standards.n: Motion to Strike Juror for Cause
standards.note: a reasonable doubt about the juror's impartiality; distinct from peremptory; preserve by exhausting peremptories + requesting more
pure questions of law, MTD, MSJ, contract interpretation — no deference
discretionary rulings (new trial, evidence, continuances) — reversed only if arbitrary/unreasonable
factual findings reviewed for CSE — affirmed if supported by such evidence as a reasonable mind would accept
